item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
IT CRIED WOLF
LIKE I'M PROBABLY NOT THE ONLY ONE WHO PICKED UP ON THAT BUT STILL I LOVE IT

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the key is that there is possibility the alarm inflated the event itself - turning a dog into anomalous canid. As soon as it was responded the event was resolved. If I am right then this article definitely has some potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, however, after reading that it was monitoring a primary school, and then reading Incident-01, I was really hoping it was a constant monitor of toys.

Incident: "X soldiers killed in combat" discover a child playing with toy soldiers.

Things of that nature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of an alarm that inflates the importance of occurrences, but the "we can ignore it, oh no, we shouldn't have ignored it" finale is kind of silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this, simple as it was, until you got round to the punchline. A hypochondriac attention-seeking alarm bell found outside a primary school is a unified and rather amusing image, but the 'Boy Who Cried Wolf' parallels in the final log were far too blunt and on the nose for me and completely destroyed the credibility of the piece. It actually ends up reading like a -J, because the entire last log is so clearly contrived to make a clever reference. The basic concept of the last log was acceptable, but the 'anomalous canids' was what pushed it into joke territory for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do wonder what would have happened if they had ignored the jam jar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit bare bones, but the way you show that the alarm is looking for attention is well done. Maybe a couple extra incidents would help the article a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is a good example of an article which definitely could include more but wouldn't necessarily benefit from it.

I think that from an in-universe perspective there would be a comment on how frequently these events occur though, seeing as there is a suggestion that the skip is controlling them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this idea a lot, but I'm not a fan of the execution. I think with more explanation or clearer examples I would like this better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Final hook definitely got me. This was interesting, humorous, and then ended on a creepy/weird note. Great job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm… quirky weird, leaves a lot to imaginations, I believe it is worth the read.
